transcript.text: So we are trying to yeah this is and also this is like a 45 minute interview and we are like five minutes or like 40 minutes is what I really looking at so it's gonna be like a very tight so whatever we can talk about we will talk about whatever we can that's fine right so If the premise of this thought process interview is you are now in a team that is trying to build an event system for a company like distribution for example distribution it's an internal product so there is a a lot of product teams inside of the platform inside of the company that is trying to build That is so that will be sending the events and then there are product deals that are willing to receive the events and do something with that so you're just trying to build an internal system to the company that access a system that sends and receives messages to people stuff like that so This is the basic line of the problem that you tried to solve we can you can ask me any number of questions ask me to explain any scenarios so you can like it's it's yours like that is no one right answer we could talk about what you think you need to build for the system we will talk about what is good but is bad what do you think why do you think you need something is it good do you see any cause you see any stuff like that so this is discussion OK

blocks.md:
# 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — delivery guarantees, ordering, and consumer scaling — checking in with you as I go. We've got ~40 minutes so I'll keep moving and let you steer the deep dives. Sound good?"

The core tension here: this is an **internal pub/sub event bus** — many product teams *publish* events, many others *subscribe* and react. The whole game is **decoupling producers from consumers** while promising a clear **delivery guarantee** (we don't silently lose events) without one slow or broken consumer backing up everyone else.

🏢 I'll target a **Google/Meta-style infra bar** — one distributed-systems problem (delivery + ordering) taken deep — since this is an internal platform/infra team. Tell me if it's Amazon (I'll make cost/ops/multi-tenancy first-class) and I'll re-tilt.

**Functional (core flows — "above the line"):**
- **Publish:** A producer team can publish an event to a named **topic** (an event stream/channel), and get an ack that it's durably stored.
- **Subscribe & consume:** A consumer team can create a **subscription** to a topic and receive every event, with multiple consumer instances sharing the load (a **consumer group**).
- **Reliable delivery with retries + DLQ:** The system delivers at-least-once, retries on failure with backoff, and parks poison events in a **dead-letter queue** so one bad event doesn't block the stream.
- **Topic / subscription management:** A team can self-serve create a topic, set retention, register a schema, and grant other teams access.

**Below the line (out of scope for today):** exactly-once end-to-end into consumer side-effects, full stream-processing/joins (Flink-style), cross-cloud federation, a UI/console, billing/chargeback (I'll mention it under cost).

**Non-Functional (the system should…):**
- **Be highly available for publishes — 4 nines (99.99%)** on the publish path. A publish failing is the worst outcome (the event is gone at the source); consume can lag briefly.
- **Durable — effectively zero event loss** once ack'd: replicate every event across ≥3 nodes/AZs before acking.
- **Deliver at-least-once**, with **per-key ordering** within a partition; consumers are expected to be idempotent. Global total order is *not* a goal.
- **Low publish latency — p99 < ~50ms** to ack; end-to-end (publish→delivered) p99 in the low seconds under healthy consumers.
- **Scale + multi-tenant isolation:** thousands of topics, hundreds of teams; one noisy tenant can't starve another.
- **Replayable:** consumers can rewind to a past offset/timestamp within the retention window.

> 💬 "The thing I most want to protect is the publish path and durability — once we say 'got it,' that event must never vanish. I'll happily trade a second or two of consumer lag for that."

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** true exactly-once into consumer side effects is impossible without consumer cooperation — I'll give at-least-once + idempotency keys and be explicit about it rather than pretend.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So this is an internal event bus — product teams publish events to topics, other teams subscribe and consume them, and our job is to decouple the two sides and never lose an event we've acknowledged. The core flows are publish, subscribe-and-consume as a group, reliable delivery with retries and a dead-letter queue, and self-serve topic management. Non-functionally I care most about a rock-solid, highly-available publish path and durability — at-least-once delivery with per-key ordering, and isolation so one noisy team can't hurt another. I'm explicitly *not* promising global ordering or end-to-end exactly-once, and I'll say why when we get there."

# 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

Questions I'd ask, and why each one moves the design:

- **Push or pull delivery to consumers?** Push (webhook/gRPC to the consumer's endpoint) is easier for app teams but harder to flow-control; pull (consumer polls, Kafka-style) gives backpressure for free. → *Drives the whole consumer architecture.* I'll support **both**, pull as the core primitive.
- **Ordering requirement — global, per-key, or none?** Global total order kills throughput (single partition). → I'll assume **per-key ordering** (e.g. all events for one `user_id` in order), which lets us partition.
- **What's the durability/loss tolerance?** Financial-grade zero-loss vs best-effort telemetry changes replication and ack settings. → Assume **zero-loss for ack'd events**, mixed criticality across topics.
- **Retention window?** Hours vs days vs "infinite/replayable" drives storage cost massively. → Assume **default 7 days**, configurable per topic.
- **Payload size?** A few KB of JSON vs large blobs. → Assume **small events, ~1–5 KB**; large payloads go to blob storage and we carry a pointer (claim-check pattern).
- **Multi-region?** Single region with multi-AZ, or active-active across regions? → Assume **single region, multi-AZ** for v1; I'll note the multi-region path.

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:** internal-only (auth via service identity/mTLS, not public), at-least-once + per-key order, 7-day retention, ~1–5 KB JSON events, single region / 3 AZs, pull-based core with an optional push delivery service.

> 💬 "I'm going to assume per-key ordering rather than global ordering — that single assumption is what lets us partition and scale, so I want to confirm it early."

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Before I size it — are you happy with at-least-once plus per-key ordering as the contract, or do you want me to design for stricter global ordering or exactly-once? That changes the core a lot."

# 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll defer a full estimation and just compute the handful of numbers that actually drive a decision — peak publish QPS, storage footprint, and per-broker throughput to decide partition/broker counts. Let me run the math.

blocks.name: run_command

input.command:
python3 -c "
# Assumptions
events_per_day = 50_000_000_000   # 50B events/day internal (big company, many teams)
sec_per_day = 86400
avg_qps = events_per_day/sec_per_day
peak = avg_qps*3   # peak factor
event_size = 2_000  # bytes (2KB avg)
fanout = 3          # avg subscriptions per topic -> read amplification

print('avg publish QPS      ', f'{avg_qps:,.0f}')
print('peak publish QPS     ', f'{peak:,.0f}')
print('peak publish MB/s    ', f'{peak*event_size/1e6:,.0f}')
print('peak deliver QPS x3  ', f'{peak*fanout:,.0f}')

# storage 7 day retention, replication factor 3
retention_days = 7
rep = 3
raw_per_day = events_per_day*event_size
store_7d_rep = raw_per_day*retention_days*rep
print('raw/day TB           ', f'{raw_per_day/1e12:,.1f}')
print('7d x3 replication PB ', f'{store_7d_rep/1e15:,.2f}')

# broker sizing: a Kafka-like broker ~ 100-200K msg/s, ~100MB/s net
per_broker_msgs = 150_000
print('brokers for publish  ', f'{peak/per_broker_msgs:,.0f}')

# partitions: per-partition ~ a few MB/s; target 10MB/s/partition
per_part = 10e6/event_size
print('min partitions       ', f'{peak/per_part:,.0f}')
"

blocks.output:
avg publish QPS       578,704
peak publish QPS      1,736,111
peak publish MB/s     3,472
peak deliver QPS x3   5,208,333
raw/day TB            100.0
7d x3 replication PB  2.10
brokers for publish   12
min partitions        347

blocks.md:
| Metric | Rounded, say-out-loud | Drives |
|---|---|---|
| Avg publish | ~580K events/s | baseline |
| **Peak publish** | **~1.7M events/s** | **the binding number** |
| Peak ingress bandwidth | ~3.5 GB/s | NIC/broker count |
| Peak delivery (×3 fan-out) | ~5M events/s | read tier sizing |
| Raw data/day | ~100 TB/day | storage |
| Stored (7d × 3 replicas) | ~2 PB | cost driver |
| Brokers (publish) | ~12+ (call it ~20 w/ headroom) | cluster size |
| Min partitions | ~350 (round to 512–1024) | parallelism unit |

**QPS** *(queries/events per second)*, **TTL** *(time-to-live — auto-expiry)*, replication factor 3 *(every event written to 3 machines in 3 availability zones before we ack, so two can die and we still have it)*.

**The ONE number that forces a decision: ~1.7M events/s peak.** A single broker tops out ~150K msgs/s, so we're *forced* to partition and shard across ~15–20 brokers — there is no single-node or single-queue answer. The flip threshold: under ~100K/s I'd just run one beefy managed queue (SQS/a single Kafka topic) and skip all the partitioning machinery.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Peak is around 1.7 million events a second, and a single broker handles maybe 150K — so the headline is that no single node survives this, we're partitioning and sharding from day one across roughly twenty brokers. Fan-out to consumers roughly triples that on the read side, so the read tier is the bigger beast. Storage is honestly a non-issue — a couple petabytes for a week with replication, which is cheap disk — so I'll spend my design budget on throughput and delivery, not storage."

# 4. Core Entities

First draft of the nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later:

- **Topic** — a named, durable event stream a team publishes to (e.g. `payments.charge_succeeded`).
- **Partition** — a shard of a topic; the unit of ordering and parallelism. Events with the same key land on the same partition.
- **Event / Message** — one immutable record: key, payload, timestamp, offset, headers.
- **Producer** — a team/service publishing events (identified by service identity).
- **Subscription** — a named consumer's interest in a topic, with its own independent cursor.
- **Consumer Group** — a set of consumer instances sharing a subscription's partitions for parallelism.
- **Offset / Cursor** — per-subscription, per-partition position marking "consumed up to here."
- **Dead-Letter Queue (DLQ)** — where events go after exhausting retries.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The key nouns are topics, which are split into partitions for ordering and scale; events; producers; and on the read side, subscriptions and consumer groups, each tracking its own offset into each partition. There's also a dead-letter queue for poison messages. That's the first-draft model — I'll nail down the exact fields when we get to the data model."

# 5. API / Interface

Going one-by-one through the functional requirements. Internal RPC (gRPC) in practice, shown REST-style for clarity. **Caller identity comes from mTLS/service identity — never from a client-supplied `team_id`.**

**Publish** (FR1):
```
POST /v1/topics/{topic}/events
Headers: Idempotency-Key: <producer-event-id>
Body: { "key": "user_123", "payload": {...}, "headers": {...} }
→ 200 { "partition": 7, "offset": 88123, "event_id": "..." }
```
Why POST: it creates/appends an event. The **Idempotency-Key** lets us dedup producer retries so a network hiccup doesn't double-publish.

**Create subscription** (FR2):
```
POST /v1/topics/{topic}/subscriptions
Body: { "name": "fraud-service", "start": "latest|earliest|<ts>", "delivery": "pull|push", "endpoint": "..." }
→ 201 { "subscription_id": "..." }
```

**Consume — pull** (FR2):
```
POST /v1/subscriptions/{sub}/poll   Body: { "max": 500 }
→ 200 { "events": [...], "ack_token": "..." }
POST /v1/subscriptions/{sub}/ack    Body: { "ack_token": "..." }   // commit offset
```
Why two calls: we deliver, the consumer processes, then **acks** to advance the offset. No ack → redelivery (that's how we get at-least-once).

**Manage** (FR4):
```
PUT /v1/topics/{topic}        { "partitions": 512, "retention_days": 7, "schema_id": "..." }
POST /v1/topics/{topic}/grants { "team": "fraud", "role": "consumer" }
```

> 💬 "Notice the poll-then-ack split — that two-phase handshake is exactly what gives us at-least-once delivery, because we only advance the cursor after the consumer confirms it processed the batch."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Producers POST events with an idempotency key so retries don't duplicate. Consumers create a named subscription, then loop on poll-process-ack — and crucially we only advance their offset on the ack, which is what guarantees at-least-once. Topic and subscription management are simple CRUD with access grants. Everything authenticates by service identity at the transport layer, so I never trust a caller-supplied team id."

# 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

Palette: client = caller apps, svc = our services, async = brokers/logs, store = datastores, ext = external.

### 6.1 "A producer can publish an event to a topic"

The foundation: accept an event, route it to the right partition, durably replicate it, ack.

| Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Publish Gateway | Stateless gRPC svc behind L4 LB | Auth, validate schema, route by key→partition | Extra hop (~1ms) |
| Event Log / Broker | **Kafka-style partitioned log** | Append-only = high write throughput; partitions = ordering + parallelism; replication = durability | Operationally heavy |
| Metadata store | **Postgres** | Topic/partition/schema config, strongly consistent, low volume | Not on hot path (cached) |

**Kafka-style log** *(an append-only, replicated commit log split into partitions — events are appended to the end and never edited; it's the durable buffer between producers and consumers so a slow consumer never blocks a producer).*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  P[Producer team]:::client -->|1 publish event + idem key| GW[Publish Gateway]:::svc
  GW -->|2 schema + auth check| META[(Metadata DB)]:::store
  GW -->|3 key hash to partition| LOG[Event Log - partitioned]:::async
  LOG -->|4 replicate x3 across AZ| LOG
  LOG -->|5 ack offset| GW
  GW -->|6 200 partition+offset| P
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
```
↳ reuses existing: none yet — this is the foundation.

1. Producer publishes event + `Idempotency-Key` to the **Publish Gateway**.
2. Gateway authenticates (mTLS identity) and validates payload against the topic's registered schema (config cached from Metadata DB).
3. Gateway hashes the event **key** → picks a partition (`hash(key) % numPartitions`), so same key = same partition = ordered.
4. The log **appends** and **replicates to 3 replicas across 3 AZs**; leader waits for in-sync replica acks (`acks=all`). **State change:** event now durable, assigned an immutable **offset**.
5–6. Leader returns the offset; gateway returns 200. If the producer retries, the idempotency key dedups it.

> 💬 "I hash the event key to choose a partition — that's the trick that gives us ordering per key while still spreading load across the whole cluster."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "On publish, a stateless gateway authenticates the caller, validates the event against the topic schema, then hashes the event key to pick a partition and appends to a replicated commit log. We replicate to three brokers across three availability zones and only ack once they've all got it — that's our zero-loss promise. The key-to-partition hash is doing double duty: it spreads load *and* keeps all events for a given key in order."

### 6.2 "A consumer can subscribe and consume as a group"

Now the read side: a subscription with multiple consumer instances splitting the partitions.

| Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Subscription/Offset store | **Kafka `__offsets`-style log + Redis cache** | Per-sub, per-partition cursor; high write rate on ack | Eventual on cache, source-of-truth in log |
| Group Coordinator | Broker-side coordinator | Assigns partitions to live consumers, rebalances on join/leave | Rebalance pauses |
| Pull API | gRPC long-poll | Backpressure for free — consumer pulls at its own rate | Consumer must poll |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  LOG[Event Log - partitioned]:::async -->|2 fetch from offset| BR[Broker / Pull API]:::svc
  C1[Consumer A - inst 1]:::client -->|1 poll| BR
  C2[Consumer A - inst 2]:::client -->|1 poll| BR
  BR -->|3 events batch| C1
  BR -->|3 events batch| C2
  C1 -->|4 ack offset| BR
  BR -->|5 commit cursor| OFF[(Offset store + Redis)]:::store
  COORD[Group Coordinator]:::svc -.->|assign partitions| C1
  COORD -.->|assign partitions| C2
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
```
↳ reuses existing: **Event Log** (from §6.1).

1. Each consumer instance in group "fraud-service" polls; the **Coordinator** has assigned each instance a disjoint set of partitions (instance 1 → partitions 0–255, instance 2 → 256–511).
2. Broker reads events from each partition starting at the subscription's committed **offset**.
3. Returns a batch + an ack token.
4. Consumer processes, then acks. **State change:** committed offset advances.
5. Offset persisted to the offset store (Redis for fast reads, durable log as truth). A different subscription on the same topic has its *own* offset — that's how multiple teams independently consume the same stream.

> 💬 "Two different teams subscribing to the same topic each get their own cursor, so they read the full stream independently and never interfere — that's the core of pub/sub."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "A subscription is a named cursor over the topic. A consumer group is several instances of that subscriber, and a coordinator splits the partitions across them so they parallelize without overlap. Each instance loops poll-process-ack, and we only advance the stored offset on ack. Because every subscription keeps its own offset, ten different teams can read the same topic at their own pace — that independence is the whole point of pub/sub."

### 6.3 "Reliable delivery with retries + DLQ"

The slice that makes it trustworthy: handle a consumer that fails or chokes on a poison event.

| Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Retry tracker | Per-message delivery count in offset metadata | Know when to give up | Small bookkeeping |
| Retry topics | **Tiered delay topics** (5s, 1m, 10m) | Backoff without blocking the main partition | Extra topics |
| DLQ | Dedicated per-subscription topic | Park poison events, alert owner, allow replay after fix | Needs monitoring |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  BR[Broker / Pull API]:::svc -->|1 deliver| C[Consumer]:::client
  C -->|2a ack ok| BR
  C -->|2b nack / timeout| RT[Retry topics 5s-1m-10m]:::async
  RT -->|3 redeliver after delay| BR
  RT -->|4 max retries exceeded| DLQ[DLQ topic]:::async
  DLQ -->|5 alert owner| OWN[Owning team]:::ext
  DLQ -->|6 replay after fix| BR
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe;
```
↳ reuses existing: **Broker/Pull API** (§6.2), **Event Log** (§6.1).

1. Broker delivers a batch.
2a. Ack → offset advances. 2b. Nack or visibility-timeout expiry → event is republished to a **retry topic** with a delay.
3. After the delay, it's redelivered. Delivery count increments each time.
4. After N retries (say 5) → moved to the **DLQ**. **State change:** main partition's offset advances past it, so the poison event no longer blocks the stream.
5–6. The DLQ alerts the owning team; once they fix the bug they replay the DLQ back through the consumer.

> ⚠️ **Trap:** the classic failure is letting a single poison event sit at the head of an ordered partition and block *all* events behind it forever. The retry-topic + DLQ pattern is exactly what unblocks the partition while preserving the bad event.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "If a consumer fails or times out without acking, we don't lose the event and we don't block the partition — we shunt it to a tiered retry topic with backoff, five seconds, then a minute, then ten. After a handful of failures it lands in a dead-letter queue that pages the owning team, and they can replay it after fixing their bug. The key insight is the main partition's offset moves past the poison event, so one bad message never stalls the whole stream behind it."

---

**Final (high-level)** — the reconciled union of all three slices:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph Clients
    P[Producer team]:::client
    C[Consumer group]:::client
  end
  subgraph WritePath[Publish path]
    GW[Publish Gateway]:::svc
    META[(Metadata DB)]:::store
  end
  subgraph Core[Durable core]
    LOG[Event Log - partitioned x3 AZ]:::async
    RT[Retry topics]:::async
    DLQ[DLQ topic]:::async
  end
  subgraph ReadPath[Consume path]
    BR[Broker / Pull API]:::svc
    COORD[Group Coordinator]:::svc
    OFF[(Offset store + Redis)]:::store
  end
  P -->|publish| GW
  GW -->|schema/auth| META
  GW -->|append| LOG
  LOG --> BR
  BR -->|poll| C
  C -->|ack| BR
  BR --> OFF
  COORD -.->|assign| C
  C -->|nack| RT
  RT --> BR
  RT -->|max retries| DLQ
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
```

Load-bearing schema: **Event Log** partitioned by `hash(key)`, each event = `{offset, key, payload, headers, ts}`; **Offset store** keyed by `(subscription_id, partition) → committed_offset`.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "End to end: producers publish through a stateless gateway into a partitioned, triple-replicated log — that's our durable spine. Consumers in groups pull from the log, each subscription tracking its own offset, acking to advance. Failures flow through retry topics into a dead-letter queue without blocking anyone. The log decouples the two sides completely — producers never wait on consumers, and consumers can rewind and replay within retention."

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. The three areas I'd go deep on are delivery guarantees and idempotency, partition/ordering under hot keys, and consumer rebalancing. Which is most interesting to you — or is there a corner you want to poke at?"

# 7. Data Model & Storage

| Entity | Key fields | Store | Why (access pattern) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Event** | offset (PK), key, payload, headers, ts | **Partitioned commit log** (Kafka-style) | Append-only sequential writes + sequential reads-from-offset; replication = durability. No random access needed. |
| **Topic** | topic_id, name, partitions, retention, schema_id | **Postgres** | Strongly consistent config, low volume, read-mostly (cached) |
| **Subscription** | sub_id, topic, group, start_pos | **Postgres** | Config; rarely changes |
| **Offset/Cursor** | (sub_id, partition) → offset, delivery_count | **Durable offset log + Redis cache** | Extremely high write rate (every ack); needs fast read on poll; truth in log |
| **Schema** | schema_id, version, definition | **Postgres + cache** | Versioned, read on every publish (cached) |
| **DLQ event** | original event + failure metadata | **Commit log (separate topic)** | Same as events; needs replay |

Partition/shard key: **`hash(event.key)`** for events (ordering + spread); `topic_id` for metadata.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Events live in an append-only replicated commit log because the access pattern is pure sequential write and sequential read from an offset — no random lookups — and the log gives us durability and replay for free. Topic, subscription and schema config go in Postgres because it's low-volume, read-mostly, and needs strong consistency, and we cache it so it's never on the hot path. Offsets are the spicy one — they're written on every single ack at huge rate, so the source of truth is a compacted log but we keep a Redis cache for fast reads on poll. Nothing here needs a general-purpose database on the hot path; the log *is* the database."

# 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to "what's the delivery contract and where can an event be lost or duplicated?" — re-derive forward from there.

### How do we guarantee no event loss *and* handle duplicates (delivery semantics)?

**#### Bad: Fire-and-forget / single replica.** Gateway writes to one broker, acks immediately. **Challenge:** broker dies before replicating → event gone, despite the ack. Violates our zero-loss SLO. ⚠️ **Trap:** acking before replication (`acks=1`) to chase latency — silently loses data under failover.

**#### Good: Replicated append + at-least-once.** `acks=all` to 3 AZ replicas before ack; consumer poll-process-ack. **Challenge:** at-least-once means **duplicates** — a consumer that crashes after processing but before acking re-processes the batch on restart. Acceptable only if consumers are idempotent.

**#### Great: Replicated log + producer idempotency + consumer dedup keys.**
- **Producer side:** every event carries an `Idempotency-Key` (producer event id). The gateway dedups within a partition window, so a producer retry after a timeout doesn't create a second event.
- **Consumer side:** we hand each event a stable `event_id`; consumers dedup on it (a processed-id set with TTL, or natural idempotency via upsert). This makes their *effects* exactly-once even though *delivery* is at-least-once.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  P[Producer]:::client -->|event + idem key| GW[Gateway]:::svc
  GW -->|dedup window| LOG[Log acks=all x3]:::async
  LOG -->|event_id| C[Consumer]:::client
  C -->|seen event_id?| DD[(Dedup store TTL)]:::store
  DD -->|no - process| C
  DD -->|yes - skip| C
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
```

**Read-your-own-writes / namespace:** the `event_id` namespace is `(partition, offset)` — globally unique by construction, no collision between producers. Why this is what real systems do: Kafka's idempotent producer + consumer-side idempotency is exactly this; true end-to-end exactly-once needs transactional sinks, which we can't force on every team.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "We replicate to three AZs and only ack after all three have it — that's zero loss. Delivery is at-least-once, so duplicates are possible, and I handle that on both ends: producers send an idempotency key so a retry doesn't double-publish, and every event has a stable id so consumers can dedup and make their side-effects effectively exactly-once. I'm honest that *true* exactly-once needs the consumer's database in the loop — I give them the id to make that easy rather than pretend the bus does it alone."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not exactly-once delivery?":** "Exactly-once *delivery* over a network is impossible — the ack can always be lost. What's achievable is exactly-once *processing* via idempotency, so I push the dedup key to the edges and own at-least-once in the middle."

### How do we keep ordering while scaling — and survive hot keys?

**#### Good: Partition by key hash.** `hash(key) % N` → same key, same partition, ordered. Scales to N partitions. **Challenge:** a **hot key** (e.g. one giant tenant's `org_id`) overloads its single partition — that partition's broker is the bottleneck at, say, 150K/s while others idle. Also, changing N reshuffles all keys.

**#### Great: Sticky hashing + hot-key splitting + over-partitioning.**
- **Over-partition up front** (1024 partitions) so we can add brokers without repartitioning — partitions move, keys don't.
- **Detect hot keys** and either (a) split a hot key across sub-partitions with a composite key `key#bucket` *if* strict per-key order isn't required for that topic, or (b) give the whale tenant its own dedicated partitions.
- Per-key order is preserved for everyone else; the hot key trades some ordering for throughput by explicit opt-in.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  E[Events]:::async -->|hash key| H{Hot key?}:::svc
  H -->|no| PN[Partition N - ordered]:::async
  H -->|yes| SP[Sub-partitions key#bucket]:::async
  SP --> B1[Broker A]:::svc
  SP --> B2[Broker B]:::svc
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
```

The binding bottleneck: **one partition ≈ one broker core for ordering ≈ ~150K/s ceiling.** Relief = sub-partition the hot key → new ceiling = N×150K. Cost: that key loses strict total order.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "I partition by hashing the event key, which keeps each key ordered while spreading load. The danger is a hot key — one huge tenant hammering a single partition while the rest idle. So I over-partition from the start to avoid repartitioning later, detect hot keys, and for those I either give them dedicated partitions or split them across buckets if that topic can tolerate it. It's an explicit trade: that one key gives up strict ordering to get throughput, and everyone else keeps their guarantees."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just add partitions when hot?":** "Adding partitions changes `hash % N` and reshuffles every key's assignment, breaking ordering mid-stream. Over-provisioning partitions and moving *partitions* between brokers avoids the reshuffle."

### How do consumer groups rebalance without dropping or double-delivering?

**#### Bad: Stop-the-world rebalance.** On any consumer join/leave, revoke all partitions and reassign. **Challenge:** every consumer pauses ("rebalance storm") — a flapping consumer causes repeated global stalls; latency spikes.

**#### Great: Incremental cooperative rebalancing + heartbeats + lease.**
- Consumers heartbeat to the coordinator; a missed heartbeat (lease expiry) frees only *that* consumer's partitions.
- **Cooperative rebalance:** only the affected partitions move; the rest keep flowing.
- A partition is owned by exactly one consumer at a time via a **lease/generation id**, so no two consumers process the same partition (no double-delivery beyond the at-least-once redelivery window).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  C1[Consumer 1]:::client -->|heartbeat| CO[Coordinator]:::svc
  C2[Consumer 2]:::client -->|heartbeat| CO
  CO -->|lease gen-7 part 0-255| C1
  CO -->|lease gen-7 part 256-511| C2
  C2 -.->|dies, lease expires| CO
  CO -->|reassign only 256-511| C1
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
```

This coordinator is a **single global coordinator** per group — its ceiling is just metadata ops (low); on coordinator failure a standby takes over from the replicated group state; while it's down, consumption *continues* on existing leases, only rebalances pause. The invariant it protects: one-owner-per-partition.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Each consumer holds a lease on its partitions and heartbeats to a coordinator. If one dies, only its partitions get reassigned — everyone else keeps flowing, that's cooperative rebalancing instead of stopping the world. A generation-stamped lease guarantees exactly one consumer owns a partition at a time, so we don't double-process beyond the normal redelivery window. The coordinator only does lightweight metadata work and has a hot standby, and even if it blips, existing consumers keep going — only rebalances pause."

> 🧠 **If they ask "what if a consumer is just slow, not dead?":** "Visibility timeout — if it doesn't ack within the lease window we assume failure and redeliver. That's why consumers must be idempotent; a slow consumer can cause a duplicate, never a loss."

### How does push delivery handle a slow/down consumer endpoint?
Pull gives backpressure free; **push** doesn't. For push subscriptions we use a delivery service with **per-endpoint concurrency limits + circuit breaker**: if a consumer's endpoint 500s or times out, we open the breaker, stop hammering it, and fall back to retry topics/DLQ. This keeps one dead endpoint from consuming all delivery threads (bulkhead isolation).

> 🎙️ **Script:** "For teams that want events pushed to their endpoint, I put a delivery service in front with per-endpoint concurrency caps and a circuit breaker, so a slow or dead consumer endpoint gets backed off and dead-lettered instead of soaking up all our delivery capacity and hurting other tenants."

# 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

- **Availability:** publish path designed for **4 nines** — stateless gateways behind LB across AZs, brokers replicated 3× across 3 AZs; loss of one AZ = no data loss, no publish outage (leader re-election in seconds).
- **Per-dependency degradation:**
  - *Metadata DB down* → serve from config cache; publishes keep flowing, only new topic creation pauses.
  - *Redis offset cache down* → fall back to the durable offset log (slower polls, still correct).
  - *A broker/AZ down* → in-sync replica is promoted; producers retry to new leader.
  - *A consumer down* → lease expires, partitions reassigned; events buffered in log (retention), no loss.
- **RPO/RTO:** ack'd events **RPO ≈ 0** (replicated before ack); RTO seconds for leader failover. Metadata: RPO ≤ minutes (Postgres WAL + PITR), tested restore quarterly.
- **Multi-AZ vs region:** multi-AZ default. Multi-region = async mirror (MirrorMaker-style) to a DR region; failover is a deliberate trigger (region-wide outage), with the caveat of bounded replication lag (some in-flight events may need replay).
- **Cost:** dominant driver is **brokers + their disk/network** (~20 brokers, ~2 PB replicated SSD, high inter-AZ bandwidth) — call it the bulk of the bill; storage itself is cheap, **cross-AZ replication bandwidth** is the sneaky cost. I'd add per-team **chargeback** by volume to control demand.
- **Ops:** SLO **burn-rate alerts** on publish error rate + p99, **consumer lag** per subscription (the #1 health signal — lag growing = consumer falling behind), DLQ depth alerts, distributed tracing via event headers, runbooks for broker failover and DLQ replay.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Durability comes from replicating across three AZs before we ack, so an AZ loss is zero data loss and a few seconds of failover. Every dependency degrades gracefully — metadata and offset caches have durable fallbacks, dead consumers just let their backlog sit safely in the log. The metric I watch hardest is consumer lag per subscription — that's the early warning that someone's falling behind. Biggest cost is brokers and especially cross-AZ replication bandwidth, so I'd add volume-based chargeback to keep teams honest."

# 10. Trade-off Ledger

- **At-least-once + idempotency (not exactly-once delivery).** Gave up: zero duplicates out of the box; pushed dedup work onto consumers. Reverses if: we owned the consumer sinks transactionally — then we could offer end-to-end exactly-once. Tied to the at-least-once SLO.
- **Per-key ordering only (not global).** Gave up: total order. Bought: horizontal scale to 1.7M/s. Reverses if: a topic genuinely needs global order *and* is low-volume — then a single partition is fine.
- **Pull-first (push as add-on).** Gave up: simplicity for app teams who just want a webhook. Bought: backpressure and isolation. Reverses if: most consumers are tiny/bursty and can't run a polling loop — then push-first with a robust delivery tier.
- **Single region v1.** Gave up: regional DR. Reverses if: the business needs survive-a-region — add async cross-region mirroring, accepting bounded replay on failover.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The three calls I'd flag: at-least-once instead of exactly-once delivery — I pushed dedup to the edges deliberately; per-key instead of global ordering, which is what buys our scale; and pull-first delivery for backpressure. Each one flips if the assumptions change — a low-volume topic can have global order, and a region requirement adds cross-region mirroring."

# 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**1. Why a log (Kafka-style) instead of a queue (SQS/RabbitMQ)?**
A log retains events after consumption and supports *multiple independent subscribers* each with their own offset, plus replay — exactly what pub/sub with many teams needs. A traditional queue deletes on ack and is point-to-point, so you'd need a copy per consumer. The log also gives sequential-write throughput a queue can't match at 1.7M/s.
> 💬 "A queue deletes the message when one consumer reads it; a log keeps it so ten teams can each read the whole stream at their own pace and replay history — that's why pub/sub wants a log."

**2. What exactly happens when a consumer crashes mid-batch?**
It processed some events but never acked, so its lease eventually expires and the coordinator reassigns those partitions to another instance, which re-polls from the last *committed* offset. The already-processed events get redelivered — hence at-least-once — and consumer-side dedup on `event_id` prevents double effects. No event is lost because the offset only advances on ack.
> 💬 "We never advanced its cursor, so a healthy instance just resumes from the last commit — some events replay, dedup catches them."

**3. How do you stop one poison message blocking a partition?**
After N delivery attempts the message is moved to a retry topic and ultimately the DLQ, and the main partition's offset advances past it. So the stream keeps flowing while the bad event is parked for the owning team to inspect and replay after a fix. Without this, an ordered partition would stall forever behind the unprocessable event.
> 💬 "We let the cursor step over it into a dead-letter queue, so the stream moves on and the bad event waits safely for a human."

**4. A single tenant is 100x bigger than others — what breaks?**
Their key hashes to one partition, so that partition's broker saturates while others idle — a hot-partition bottleneck around 150K/s. I'd give that tenant dedicated partitions or split their key across buckets if the topic tolerates relaxed ordering, and meter them via chargeback. Over-partitioning up front means I can rebalance partitions to brokers without breaking key ordering.
> 💬 "One whale lands on one partition and melts it; I shard the whale across dedicated partitions and keep everyone else's ordering intact."

**5. How do you handle schema evolution so producers don't break consumers?**
A schema registry stores versioned schemas; producers validate on publish and we enforce **backward-compatible** changes (add optional fields, never remove/retype). Consumers read with their known schema version. Breaking changes require a new topic version, not an in-place change, so old consumers keep working.
> 💬 "We version schemas and only allow backward-compatible changes inline; a breaking change is a new topic, never a silent break."

**6. What's your ordering guarantee precisely, and why not global?**
Per-partition (per-key) total order: all events sharing a key are delivered in publish order. Global order would force a single partition and cap us near one broker's throughput — unacceptable at 1.7M/s. Most use cases only need "events for *this* entity in order," which per-key satisfies.
> 💬 "Everything for one user is in order; across users there's no global clock — and that's the only way to scale."

**7. How do consumers replay / reprocess after a bug?**
Because the log retains events for the retention window, a consumer can reset its subscription's offset to an earlier position or timestamp and re-consume. We expose `start: earliest|<timestamp>` on the subscription and an offset-reset admin call. They should be idempotent so replay doesn't double-apply.
> 💬 "Retention plus offsets means replay is just 'move my cursor back and read again.'"

**8. How do you prevent duplicate publishes from producer retries?**
The producer attaches an idempotency key; the gateway/broker dedups within the partition so a retried publish after a lost ack maps to the same offset rather than a new event. This is Kafka's idempotent-producer mechanism — a producer-id + sequence number per partition.
> 💬 "Each publish carries a unique key, so a retry collapses onto the original instead of creating a twin."

**9. How is multi-tenancy / isolation enforced so one team can't hurt another?**
Per-topic and per-subscription **quotas** (publish rate, byte rate) enforced at the gateway and broker, plus dedicated partitions or even dedicated broker pools for the largest tenants (bulkheading). Auth via service identity with per-topic grants. Noisy-neighbor protection is rate-limit + isolation, and chargeback discourages abuse.
> 💬 "Quotas per topic at the gateway plus isolating the big tenants means a noisy team throttles itself, not the platform."

**10. What about the offset store at this scale — isn't every ack a write?**
Yes — acks are the highest write rate after publishes. We commit offsets as a compacted log (only the latest offset per key matters) and cache current values in Redis for fast poll-time reads. Compaction keeps storage bounded, and batching acks (commit every N or every few ms) cuts write amplification dramatically.
> 💬 "We batch and compact offset commits — only the latest matters — so it's cheap despite the volume."

**11. How would you extend this to cross-region / global?**
Async mirror topics to a second region (MirrorMaker-style), with each region having a home for its producers (active-active by topic ownership) and consumers reading locally. Cross-region adds replication lag, so on failover some recent events may replay — acceptable for at-least-once. Strong global ordering across regions is explicitly out.
> 💬 "Mirror topics region-to-region, read locally, accept bounded replay on failover — no global ordering promise."

**12. How do you detect a consumer that's silently falling behind?**
Consumer **lag** = latest offset minus committed offset, tracked per subscription per partition. Growing lag triggers a burn-rate alert to the owning team well before retention expires and events would be lost. It's the single most important health metric on the read side.
> 💬 "We watch the gap between the newest event and where each consumer is; if it grows, we page the team before they fall off the retention cliff."

---

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "We're building an internal pub/sub event bus. Producers publish to topics through a stateless gateway that authenticates, validates schema, and appends to a partitioned, triple-replicated commit log — we only ack after three AZs have the event, so we never lose an acknowledged event. Topics are partitioned by hashing the event key, which gives per-key ordering while scaling to our ~1.7-million-events-a-second peak across about twenty brokers. Consumers subscribe in groups; a coordinator leases partitions to instances, and each loops poll-process-ack, advancing its own offset only on ack — that's at-least-once. Duplicates are handled with producer idempotency keys and consumer-side dedup, failures flow through tiered retry topics into a dead-letter queue without blocking the stream, and consumers can replay from any offset within retention. The hardest parts are delivery semantics, hot-key partitioning, and cooperative rebalancing — and the metric I watch above all is consumer lag."

blocks.md:
Two real questions in there — let me hit the big one first, because you're clearly pushing on it: **"how sure are you that you need to build this?"**

## Build vs. Buy — I would *not* build a broker from scratch

This is the most important thing to say out loud early, and it's a seniority signal if I say it unprompted:

> 🎙️ "Before I design anything, let me be clear about what I'd build versus buy. I would *not* write a message broker from scratch — replication, leader election, the durable log, partition rebalancing — that's reinventing Kafka or Pulsar, it's years of work and I'd reproduce every durability bug they already fixed. The data plane I buy: managed Kafka, MSK, Confluent Cloud, or Google Pub/Sub. What I'm actually building is the **internal platform layer on top of it** — and *that's* the real product here."

So the line I draw is **data plane vs. control plane**:

| Layer | Build or Buy | What it is |
|---|---|---|
| **Data plane** (the log, replication, partitions, delivery) | **Buy** — managed Kafka / Confluent / Pub/Sub | The hard distributed-systems core that already exists and is battle-tested |
| **Control plane** (the "platform") | **Build** — this is our value-add | Self-serve topic provisioning, schema registry + governance, auth via the company's service identity, per-team quotas + chargeback, a golden-path client SDK, standardized retry/DLQ conventions, and lag/observability dashboards |

The insight for the interviewer: **the company's pain isn't "we need a log" — it's "every team rolls their own Kafka, with no governance, no schema discipline, no standard auth, and no lag visibility."** The internal product solves the *organizational* problem — one paved road — not the *distributed-systems* problem, which is already solved.

> 🎙️ "The value of an internal event system isn't the broker — it's that today every team spins up their own, with no schema governance, no consistent auth, no shared observability. So what I build is the paved road: self-serve topic creation, a schema registry that enforces compatibility, access grants tied to our service identity, quotas and chargeback so one team can't starve another, and a standard SDK so every team gets idempotency, retries, and DLQ for free. The broker underneath is bought."

🧠 **When *would* you actually build the broker?** Only at extreme scale where nothing off-the-shelf fits, or unique licensing/cost reasons — LinkedIn built Kafka in 2011 because nothing existed. Today it exists, so building it is almost always the wrong call. Saying *that* shows you know the history, not just the buzzword.

So everything in my earlier design — the partitioned replicated log, consumer groups, offsets — still stands, but I'd frame it as **"these are the Kafka/Pub/Sub primitives I'm building my platform around,"** not "components I'm implementing."

## Multi-region: US / Canada / Mexico

Given you're deployed in three regions, my answer is **regional clusters, not one global cluster** — and I'd say it decisively:

> 🎙️ "I'd run an independent cluster per region — US, Canada, Mexico — and keep producers and consumers region-local by default. Two reasons: latency, since publishing across regions would blow our 50ms ack budget, and data residency — Canada and Mexico have data-locality laws, so an event generated in Mexico should stay in Mexico unless someone explicitly needs it elsewhere."

The model:
- **Each topic has a home region.** Most events are produced and consumed in the same region — no cross-region hop, low latency, compliant by default.
- **Mirror only the topics that genuinely need to be global.** If a US team needs Canada's events, we set up **mirroring** *(an async copy of a topic into another region's cluster — Kafka MirrorMaker or Pub/Sub's built-in)* for that specific topic. Don't mirror everything — that's expensive cross-region bandwidth and a compliance headache.
- **Avoid multi-master on the same topic** — two regions writing the same topic creates ordering and conflict nightmares. One writer region per topic; others read a mirror.
- **DR falls out of this for free:** the mirror is also your disaster-recovery copy. If a region goes down, consumers fail over to the mirror and replay, accepting some bounded replication lag (we're at-least-once, so replay is safe).

> 💬 "Default everything region-local for latency and data residency, mirror only the handful of topics that truly need to cross borders, and that same mirror doubles as your DR copy."

⚠️ **Trap to avoid here:** treating "multi-region" as "one giant global cluster spanning three regions." That gives you cross-region replication on the *hot publish path* — slow acks, and a partition leader that might live in another country. Regional clusters + selective mirroring is the right shape.

---

And yes — your structure (functional → non-functional → APIs → high-level diagram) and your entity list (topic, partition, event, producer, subscription, consumer group, offset, DLQ) are exactly right. You're going in the right direction. The one thing I'd weave in early, since the interviewer just handed it to you, is the **build-vs-buy framing** — say it before you draw the broker internals, so they know you're building a *platform*, not reimplementing Kafka.

Want me to fold this into an updated requirements/scope section, or move on to fleshing out the APIs?

blocks.md:
Great example — the droplet lifecycle (created → resized → deleted, billing sums the usage between them) is the perfect lens, because it forces the two orchestration questions: **how do I organize topics across the whole company, and how do I run that as a self-serve platform.** Let me take them in order.

## 1. Topic taxonomy — the key is *who owns it and what it represents*

You're right that "everyone pushes to one topic" is bad (no isolation, no schema, no access control, one firehose) — but so is "a topic per consumer" (then the producer has to know all consumers, which kills the decoupling). The rule:

> 🎙️ "Topics are owned by the **producer** and organized around the **entity and its events**, never around the consumer. The compute team owns the droplet topics; billing, analytics, and fraud are all just subscribers. The producer never knows or cares who's listening — that's the whole point of the bus."

I'd use a **hierarchical namespace**: `<domain>.<entity>.<event>` —

```
compute.droplet.*      ← owned by the Compute team
  compute.droplet.created
  compute.droplet.resized
  compute.droplet.deleted
storage.volume.*       ← owned by the Storage team
billing.invoice.*      ← owned by the Billing team
```

Each product team owns its namespace. Onboarding a new product = it claims a namespace and starts publishing — no coordination with anyone else. That's how it scales to hundreds of teams without a central bottleneck.

## 2. The critical sub-decision your example forces: one topic or three?

This is where your billing example gets interesting. Should droplet created/resized/deleted be **three topics or one**?

> 🎙️ "For billing to work, it has to see created, then resized, then deleted for the *same droplet*, *in that order* — otherwise it bills the wrong size for the wrong window. So I'd put the whole droplet lifecycle in **one topic, `compute.droplet.lifecycle`, partitioned by `droplet_id`**. That guarantees all events for one droplet land on one partition in order, so billing replays the timeline correctly. If I split them into three topics, I'd have *no* ordering guarantee across them — billing might see the delete before the resize and bill garbage."

That's the direct payoff of the ordering decision from earlier: **the partition key is `droplet_id`, the event type is a field inside the event.** This is the single most important orchestration call in your example.

> 💬 "One topic per entity-lifecycle, keyed by the entity id — so the consumer gets the events for a given droplet in the exact order they happened. That's what makes billing correct."

⚠️ **Trap:** splitting an entity's lifecycle across multiple topics for "tidiness." It feels clean but destroys cross-event ordering, and any consumer that needs the sequence (billing, audit, state machines) breaks subtly and intermittently.

## 3. How you orchestrate it as a platform (the control plane)

This is the "service built around Kafka" the interviewer wants. The broker is bought; the **orchestration** is what we build. Four pieces:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph CP[Control plane - what we build]
    REG[Topic registry + catalog]:::svc
    SCH[Schema registry]:::svc
    ACL[Access grants]:::svc
    PROV[Self-serve provisioning]:::svc
  end
  COMPUTE[Compute team]:::client -->|1 register topic + schema| PROV
  PROV --> REG
  PROV --> SCH
  BILLING[Billing team]:::client -->|2 discover droplet topic| REG
  BILLING -->|3 request consume access| ACL
  COMPUTE -->|4 approve grant| ACL
  REG -.->|provisions| K[Kafka cluster - bought]:::async
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
```

**(a) Self-serve provisioning + a topic catalog.** Compute registers `compute.droplet.lifecycle` through a control-plane API/UI: declares the schema, retention, partition count, owner. This writes to a **catalog** so billing can later *discover* "where do droplet events live?" without Slacking the compute team.

> 🎙️ "A team self-serves a topic — declares its schema, owner, and retention — and it shows up in a searchable catalog. That catalog is how billing *finds* the droplet events instead of asking around. Discovery is the thing that actually breaks at company scale, and the catalog is what fixes it."

**(b) Schema registry = the contract between teams.** The droplet event's shape (`droplet_id`, `size`, `event_type`, `timestamp`) is a registered, versioned schema. Compute *cannot* push a breaking change that would break billing — the registry rejects incompatible changes. This is the governance that lets hundreds of teams move independently without breaking each other.

> 🎙️ "The schema is a contract. Once billing depends on the droplet event, compute can add optional fields but can't remove or retype one — the registry enforces backward compatibility. That's what lets teams ship independently without a giant cross-team coordination meeting every time."

**(c) Access grants / ownership.** Billing *requests* consume access; compute approves (or it's auto-granted for internal topics by policy). The producer owns the data and who sees it. Tied to service identity, not self-declared.

**(d) The decoupling payoff — say this out loud, it's the seniority point:**

> 🎙️ "Here's the power of orchestrating it this way: when a new team — say fraud detection or capacity planning — wants droplet events, they just subscribe through the catalog. The compute team changes *nothing* and doesn't even need to know. One producer, N consumers, added over time, zero coordination. That's the entire reason you build a central event platform instead of point-to-point integrations between every pair of teams."

## The one-line summary of the orchestration model

> 💬 "Producers own topics organized by entity-lifecycle and keyed by entity id for ordering; a control plane handles self-serve provisioning, a discovery catalog, a schema registry that enforces the contract, and access grants. Adding a new consumer never touches the producer — that decoupling is the whole product."

Want me to go deeper on the schema registry / compatibility rules, or on how billing actually reconstructs usage from the ordered event stream (the consumer-side state machine)?

blocks.md:
The schema-evolution question — this is the real test of whether the platform actually works at 50-team scale, because a producer changing its event shape is the thing that can silently break 50 consumers at once. Here's the whole story.

## First, split the change into two cases — they're completely different

> 🎙️ "The first thing I'd say is: not all changes are equal. There are **compatible** changes, which are safe and the registry just allows, and **breaking** changes, which the registry *rejects* and forces into a migration. So 'the producer wants a new structure' isn't one answer — it depends which kind it is."

| Change | Compatible? | What happens |
|---|---|---|
| **Add an optional field** (e.g. add `region` to droplet event) | ✅ Backward-compatible | Old consumers ignore the new field, new consumers read it. Just allowed. |
| Add a field with a default | ✅ Compatible | Same — defaults fill the gap |
| **Remove a field**, **rename**, **change a type** (e.g. `size: string` → `size: int`) | ❌ Breaking | Registry **rejects** the publish/CI check. Billing would crash or mis-parse |
| Make an optional field **required** | ❌ Breaking | Old producers' events now "invalid" for new consumers |

The registry enforces this **at publish time and in the producer's CI** — so a breaking change gets caught *before* it ships, not in billing's pager at 2am.

> 💬 "Adding a field is free — old consumers don't even notice. The dangerous changes are removing, renaming, or retyping a field, and the schema registry simply won't let you do those in place. It fails the build."

## How "safe" changes work mechanically

Every event carries a **schema version/id** (this is why we use Avro or Protobuf, not raw JSON — the schema id is embedded). The consumer deserializes with a **tolerant reader**: it reads the fields it knows and ignores the rest.

> 🎙️ "Each event is tagged with its schema version. Billing reads with the schema version it knows about and ignores anything new. So the compute team can add fields all day and billing keeps working untouched — that's backward compatibility doing its job."

## The hard case: a genuinely breaking change

The producer *really* needs to restructure — rename `size` to `instance_type`, change the shape. You **cannot mutate the topic's schema in place**, because 50 consumers depend on the old one. Two playbooks:

**Option A — Expand-contract (parallel change), preferred when possible:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  P[Compute producer]:::client -->|phase 1: emit BOTH old+new fields| T[droplet.lifecycle]:::async
  T --> C1[Billing - reads old]:::client
  T --> C2[Fraud - migrated to new]:::client
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
</mermaid
```
1. **Expand:** producer emits *both* the old and new fields for a deprecation window.
2. Consumers migrate to the new fields one at a time, on their own schedule — zero coordinated big-bang.
3. **Contract:** once the catalog shows no consumer reads the old field, the producer drops it.

> 🎙️ "For a breaking change I'd do expand-contract: the producer emits both the old and new shape for a while, every consumer migrates on its own timeline, and only once the catalog confirms nobody's reading the old field does the producer remove it. Nobody breaks, nobody has to coordinate a synchronized cutover."

**Option B — New topic version**, when the change is too big to coexist:
- Stand up `compute.droplet.lifecycle.v2` with the new schema.
- Producer **dual-writes** v1 and v2 during the transition.
- Consumers migrate from v1 to v2 at their own pace; deprecate and delete v1 when the catalog shows it has zero subscribers.

> 💬 "If the new structure is too different to live alongside the old, I version the topic — v2 — dual-write both, let consumers move over one by one, then retire v1 once it's empty. Same principle: no synchronized cutover across 50 teams."

## The orchestration point that ties it back

> 🎙️ "The reason this works at company scale is that the **producer owns the schema as a contract**, the **registry enforces compatibility automatically**, and the **catalog tells us who still depends on the old version** — so we know when it's safe to remove. Without those three, a producer changing its event shape is a company-wide outage. With them, it's a routine, self-paced migration. That governance is exactly the product we're building on top of Kafka — the broker doesn't give you any of it."

🧠 **If they ask "who decides what's backward-compatible?":** "The registry has a configurable compatibility mode per topic — BACKWARD, FORWARD, or FULL. Most topics are BACKWARD (new consumers can read old events), which allows adding fields and forbids removing them. The producer picks the mode when they register the topic, and CI enforces it."

That's the full lifecycle: compatible changes are automatic, breaking changes go through expand-contract or a versioned topic, and the registry + catalog are what make it safe across 50 teams without anyone coordinating. Good place to close — that schema-as-contract story is the strongest signal that you understood this as a *platform*, not just a Kafka cluster.

blocks.md:
Interview's done — nice work. Quick coach's debrief while it's fresh:

## What you did well
- **You let the interviewer steer and didn't over-talk the broker internals.** When they pushed "how sure are you that you need to build this," you didn't get defensive — that build-vs-buy pivot (buy the data plane, build the control plane) is exactly the senior signal they were fishing for.
- **You correctly landed the decoupling story** — producer owns the topic, N consumers subscribe over time with zero coordination. That's the heart of the problem and you got there.
- **You recognized the schema registry as the answer** to the evolution question on your own. That's the right instinct.

## Where to tighten (for next time)
1. **Land the conclusion faster.** On the schema-evolution answer you circled (the "suppose suppose" loops). The crisp version is three beats: *"Adding a field is safe and auto-allowed. Removing/renaming/retyping is breaking and the registry rejects it. For a real breaking change, I dual-write old+new or version the topic and let consumers migrate at their own pace."* Practice saying that in 30 seconds.
2. **Name the ordering payoff explicitly on the droplet example.** The strongest point available was: *"created→resized→deleted go in one topic keyed by droplet_id, so billing sees them in order."* You had all the pieces — say that one sentence out loud, it ties ordering + topic design + their exact example together.
3. **Drop the filler loops** ("I mean, I mean, suppose suppose"). When you feel one coming, pause instead — silence reads as senior; word-spirals read as unsure. One trick: end each thought with the *decision*, then stop. "So I'd version the topic. Next question."

## On your reverse question
Good instinct asking about team working-model — for a 2-IC startup-style team, the thing to signal back is **ownership + judgment on blast radius** (you publish an RFC when other teams are affected, otherwise you move). That's exactly what they said they want. If you get a follow-up round, lead with a story where *you* made that call.

Net: solid performance — you hit build-vs-buy, decoupling, topic taxonomy, and schema evolution, which are the four things that problem is really testing. Main growth area is **crispness of delivery**, not the technical content.

Want me to write up a one-page cheat sheet for this exact problem (the talkable lines, in order) so it's locked in for the next round?
